prompt:
You are a blind ceiling-quality judge. You receive one task and two anonymized candidate patches. Do not infer or mention model, arm, attempt, harness, source run, or author identity; judge only the task text and diffs.

Task text:
Add a run-budget feature to the `schedule` library (this repository).

Implement `Job.max_runs(n)`:

- Chainable setter that returns the Job instance, usable anywhere in the
  fluent chain, e.g. `schedule.every(5).seconds.max_runs(3).do(job_fn)` or
  `schedule.every().max_runs(3).seconds.do(job_fn)`.
- The budget counts actual executions of the job function. After the job
  has executed n times in total, the job removes itself from its scheduler
  exactly as a job returning `CancelJob` does. The nth execution itself
  still runs normally; an (n+1)-th execution must never happen.
- Applies to every execution path that runs jobs through the scheduler
  (`run_pending()` and `run_all()` included).
- Interaction with `.until(deadline)`: whichever limit is reached first
  cancels the job (first-limit-wins). The existing `.until()` semantics
  must not regress.
- After a job exhausts its budget, `Scheduler.next_run` / `idle_seconds`
  must reflect its removal.
- Validation: n must be an integer >= 1; any other value raises
  ValueError at `.max_runs(...)` call time. Calling `.max_runs()` more
  than once on the same job: the last call wins.
- Budgets are per-job: multiple jobs with different budgets must not
  interfere with each other.
- Document the new API wherever this project documents comparable Job
  methods, and keep the implementation idiomatic for this codebase.

Deliver production-quality code: the full existing test suite must keep
passing, and the new behavior must be covered by tests you add.

diff --git a/docs/examples.rst b/docs/examples.rst
index ac8f32a..291dc7f 100644
--- a/docs/examples.rst
+++ b/docs/examples.rst
@@ -244,6 +244,21 @@ Run a job until a certain time
 
 The ``until`` method sets the jobs deadline. The job will not run after the deadline.
 
+Run a job a limited number of times
+~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
+
+.. code-block:: python
+
+    import schedule
+
+    def job():
+        print('Boo')
+
+    # Run job at most three times
+    schedule.every(1).hours.max_runs(3).do(job)
+
+The ``max_runs`` method sets the maximum number of times a job can run.
+
 Time until the next execution
 ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
 Use ``schedule.idle_seconds()`` to get the number of seconds until the next job is scheduled to run.

+            schedule.run_all()

+            assert job not in schedule.jobs

+            schedule.clear()
+            mock_job.reset_mock()
+            job = every().seconds.max_runs(1).do(mock_job)
+            schedule.run_all()
+            assert schedule.default_scheduler.next_run is None
+            assert schedule.default_scheduler.idle_seconds is None
+            assert schedule.next_run() is None
+            assert schedule.idle_seconds() is None
+            assert job.run() is schedule.CancelJob
+            assert mock_job.call_count == 1

+        with mock_datetime(2020, 1, 1, 10, 0, 0):
+            schedule.clear()
+            mock_job.reset_mock()
+            every(5).seconds.max_runs(3).until("10:00:07").do(mock_job)
+            with mock_datetime(2020, 1, 1, 10, 0, 5):
+                schedule.run_pending()
+            with mock_datetime(2020, 1, 1, 10, 0, 10):
+                schedule.run_pending()

     schedule.every().minute.at(":17").do(job)
+    schedule.every(10).seconds.max_runs(3).do(job)

diff --git a/docs/examples.rst b/docs/examples.rst
index ac8f32a..9817324 100644
--- a/docs/examples.rst
+++ b/docs/examples.rst
@@ -244,6 +244,26 @@ Run a job until a certain time
 
 The ``until`` method sets the jobs deadline. The job will not run after the deadline.
 
+
+Run a job a limited number of times
+~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
+
+Use ``max_runs`` to automatically remove a job after a fixed number of executions.
+
+.. code-block:: python
+
+    import schedule
+
+    def job():
+        print('Boo')
+
+    # Run job at most three times.
+    schedule.every(5).seconds.max_runs(3).do(job)
+
+The final allowed execution runs normally before the job is removed. ``max_runs`` can
+be combined with ``until``; the job is removed when either limit is reached first.
+
+
 Time until the next execution
 ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
 Use ``schedule.idle_seconds()`` to get the number of seconds until the next job is scheduled to run.

+
+        The job is canceled after it has run ``n`` times. Calling this method more
+        than once replaces the previous limit.
+
+        :param n: Maximum number of times the job function may be run. Must be an
+            integer greater than zero.
+        :return: The invoked job instance
+        :raises ValueError: If ``n`` is not an integer greater than zero.
+        """
+        if isinstance(n, bool) or not isinstance(n, int) or n < 1:
+            raise ValueError("max_runs() requires a positive integer")
+
+        self._max_runs = n
+        if self._run_count >= n and self.scheduler is not None:
+            self.scheduler.cancel_job(self)
+        return self

+    def _is_max_runs_reached(self) -> bool:

+    def test_max_runs_counts_job_functions_that_raise(self):

+

+    def test_max_runs_are_per_job_and_update_scheduler_state(self):
+        first_job = make_mock_job("first_job")

+
+        with mock_datetime(2020, 1, 1, 11, 35, 10):
+            every().hour.max_runs(1).do(first_job)
+            every().hour.max_runs(2).do(second_job)

+            schedule.run_all()

strict_win_deltas.delta: P2 adds a private `_is_max_runs_reached()` predicate mirroring the existing `_is_overdue()` helper, while P1 repeats the full `self.max_executions is not None and self.executions_count >= self.max_executions` compound condition inline twice inside `run()` instead of factoring it out.

P2 wraps `self.job_func()` in `try/except BaseException` and explicitly calls `self.scheduler.cancel_job(self)` when the budget is exhausted by a raising call before re-raising, whereas applying P1's diff and running a `max_runs(1)` job whose function raises on its one permitted execution leaves the job present in `schedule.jobs` (verified by executing both patches), violating 'the job removes itself from its scheduler.'

P1's `max_runs()` unconditionally executes `self.executions_count = 0` on every call, so re-invoking `.max_runs(1)` after two prior runs resets the counter and lets the job execute a third time (verified by running the patched code), while P2 keeps `_run_count` intact and calls `self.scheduler.cancel_job(self)` immediately when the already-accumulated `_run_count` already meets the newly set `n`, honoring the 'must never happen' (n+1)-th-execution rule under the last-set budget.

P2 splits new coverage into six descriptively named test methods (test_max_runs, test_max_runs_applies_to_run_pending, test_max_runs_counts_job_functions_that_raise, test_max_runs_validation_and_replacement, test_max_runs_are_per_job_and_update_scheduler_state, test_max_runs_and_until_cancel_when_the_first_limit_is_reached), while P1 bundles validation, run_pending, run_all, until-interaction, next_run/idle_seconds, and per-job-independence checks into a single ~85-line test_max_runs method.
